title: Caponata
subtitle: Sicilian sweet-and-sour aubergine with capers and olives
excerpt: A Sicilian agrodolce dish of fried aubergine, celery, onion, and tomato brought together with red wine vinegar and sugar, sharpened with capers and olives, and finished with fresh basil. Served at room temperature as a starter or side dish. One of those dishes that improves significantly by a day in the fridge.
sourceNotes: Caponata is a Sicilian dish documented in Italian regional cookery literature from the eighteenth century. The sweet-and-sour agrodolce method is attributed to Arab culinary influence during the period of Arab rule in Sicily. Multiple regional Sicilian sources; no single public-domain text.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Makes an excellent large batch for a summer gathering.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make at least 2 hours ahead and serve at room temperature. The dish improves significantly after a day in the fridge.

content.text: Caponata is a room-temperature dish, not a hot one. It is closer to a relish than a vegetable side in the way it is used: spooned alongside grilled fish or meat, spread on bread, or eaten on its own as a starter. The balance between the vinegar and the sugar is what defines it — too much vinegar and it is harsh, too much sugar and it is jammy. Start with the amounts below and taste at the end; the quantities need adjusting to your vinegar's acidity.

Fry the aubergine in batches in plenty of hot oil. The temptation is to add more oil when the pan looks dry; the better approach is to keep the heat high enough that the surface of the aubergine seals quickly rather than soaking. Drain on kitchen paper. The remaining ingredients cook together in a separate pan, then everything comes together at the end.

items.prepNote: for frying, plus more as needed

content.text: Heat a large frying pan over high heat with a generous pour of the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Fry the {{aubergine}} aubergine cubes in batches for 4 to 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until golden on most sides. The pan should be hot enough that the aubergine sizzles immediately on contact. Drain on kitchen paper.

In a large saucepan, heat 2 tbsp of olive oil over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and {{celery}} celery sticks and cook for 10 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes and cook for 10 minutes until the sauce has thickened.

Add the {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar to the tomato base. Stir and cook for 2 minutes. Add the fried aubergine, {{capers}} of capers, {{olives-green}} of green olives, and {{pine-nuts}} of pine nuts. Stir well and cook for 5 minutes. Taste and adjust the balance of vinegar and sugar. Remove from the heat and allow to cool to room temperature. Stir in the {{basil}} of basil just before serving.

items.cause: Too much vinegar, or vinegar more acidic than expected
items.fix: Add more sugar a teaspoon at a time and stir through; the balance between sour and sweet is the whole point of the dish

items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or too much aubergine added at once
items.fix: Fry in small batches in properly hot oil; the surface needs to seal quickly rather than absorb oil

items.cause: Not enough time for the flavours to develop
items.fix: Make it at least 2 hours ahead; a day in the fridge produces a noticeably better result

content.text: Caponata is Sicilian rather than Italian in the mainland sense. The Arab period of Sicilian history left a mark on the island's cooking that is distinct from anything on the peninsula: the combination of sweet and sour in a savoury dish, the use of pine nuts and raisins, the layering of preserved and fresh flavours. Caponata exists in dozens of versions across the island, each town insisting its version is correct. The constants are aubergine, vinegar, sugar, and the patience to let the dish settle before eating it.
